title: Lumbee Fairness Act
stage_label: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.
plain_summary: This bill gives the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina full recognition by the federal government as a Native American tribe. The recognition means tribe members can now get all the same services and benefits that other recognized tribes receive from the government. The government can also hold land in trust for the tribe to help protect it. North Carolina will continue to handle legal matters and crimes that happen on tribal land unless the state and tribe agree to let the federal government take over some of those duties instead.
last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Lumbee Fairness Act.

sections.title: Section 2. Federal Recognition
sections.text: This section changes an older law from nineteen fifty-six about the Lumbee Indians. The changes do several things. First, it removes one section of the old law completely. Second, it reorganizes the beginning of the old law to create a findings section that lists four facts Congress agrees on about the Lumbee people. Third, it adds definitions for two important terms. The Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior, who is a government official. The Tribe means the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina or the Lumbee Indians of North Carolina. Fourth, it adds new sections that give the Lumbee Tribe official federal recognition as a Native American tribe. The new sections also explain that all federal laws that apply to other recognized tribes will now apply to the Lumbee Tribe too. The changes also say that other groups of Indians in the same area of North Carolina can still apply to be recognized as separate tribes if their members are not already part of the Lumbee Tribe. The new sections make the Lumbee Tribe eligible for all federal services and benefits that other recognized tribes receive. For people living in four specific counties in North Carolina, the government will treat them as if they live on or near an Indian reservation for the purpose of providing services. The government must figure out what services the tribe needs and report this information to Congress within two years after checking the tribe's membership roll. The Secretary of the Interior can take land and hold it in trust for the benefit of the Lumbee Tribe. Land in Robeson County, North Carolina will be treated as if it is on a reservation when the government considers trust applications. The state of North Carolina will continue to handle criminal cases and civil lawsuits that happen on Lumbee land. However, the federal government can take over some of this responsibility if North Carolina and the Lumbee Tribe make an agreement about it. Any such transfer of responsibility must wait at least two years before taking effect. Nothing in these changes affects how the Indian Child Welfare Act applies to Lumbee children.
